question.text: Which of the following correctly describes the applicability of SCRA across different derivative segments?

answer.text: SCRA applies to all exchange-traded derivatives — equity, currency, and interest rate — providing the legal framework for exchange recognition (Section 4) and derivative contract validity (Section 18A)
SCRA applies only to equity derivatives; currency derivatives are governed exclusively by FEMA and interest rate derivatives by the RBI Act
SCRA applies only to cash market securities and was never extended to cover derivative instruments
SCRA was repealed in 2002 and replaced by the SEBI Act for governing all derivative markets

explanation.text: **The Logic:** SCRA is the common legal foundation for ALL exchange-traded segments. Section 4 (exchange recognition) applies to all derivative exchanges. Section 18A (legal validity) covers all exchange-traded derivatives. FEMA and RBI Act supplement SCRA for currency and IR segments but do not replace it. **The Trap:** SCRA has NOT been repealed. It coexists with FEMA and the RBI Act. For currency derivatives, SCRA covers the exchange-traded aspect while FEMA covers the underlying forex. They complement each other. **Cross-Exam Context:** SCRA is tested across Series I, IV, VIII, and XIII. It is the single most important statute for exchange-traded derivatives — applicable universally regardless of the underlying asset. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Think of SCRA as the 'exchange umbrella' — any contract traded on a recognized exchange falls under SCRA. The underlying asset's regulator (SEBI for equity, RBI for forex/bonds) provides additional regulatory layers.
